--- SOURCE 1: Cooper pair - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair

SUMMARY:
In condensed matter physics, a **Cooper pair** or **BCS pair** (**Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer pair**) is a pair of electrons (or other fermions) bound together at low temperatures in a certain manner first described in 1956 by American physicist Leon Cooper. The Cooper pair state is responsible for superconductivity, as described in the BCS theory developed by John Bardeen, Leon Cooper, and John Schrieffer for which they shared the 1972 Nobel Prize in Physics. Therefore, unlike electrons, multiple Cooper pairs are allowed to be in the same quantum state, which is responsible for the phenomenon of superconductivity. The theory of Cooper pairs is quite general and does not depend on the specific electron-phonon interaction.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Pair of electrons bound together at low temperature, allowing for superconductivity

In [condensed matter physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Condensed_matter_physics "Condensed matter physics"), a **Cooper pair** or **BCS pair** (**Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer pair**) is a pair of [electrons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrons "Electrons") (or other [fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermion "Fermion")) bound together at [low temperatures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Low_temperatures "Low temperatures") in a certain manner first described in 1956 by American physicist [Leon Cooper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Cooper "Leon Cooper").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-1)

Description
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cooper_pair&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Cooper_pairs.jpg/250px-Cooper_pairs.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Cooper_pairs.jpg)

Schematic illustration of the Cooper pairing interaction in BCS superconductors

Cooper showed that an arbitrarily small attraction between electrons in a [metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal "Metal") can cause a paired state of electrons to have a lower energy than the [Fermi energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_energy "Fermi energy"), which implies that the pair is bound. In conventional [superconductors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superconductors "Superconductors"), this attraction is due to [Bardeen–Pines interaction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bardeen%E2%80%93Pines_interaction "Bardeen–Pines interaction"), a type of [electron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron "Electron")–[phonon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phonon "Phonon") interaction. The Cooper pair state is responsible for superconductivity, as described in the [BCS theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BCS_theory "BCS theory") developed by [John Bardeen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Bardeen "John Bardeen"), [Leon Cooper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Cooper "Leon Cooper"), and [John Schrieffer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Schrieffer "John Schrieffer") for which they shared the 1972 [Nobel Prize in Physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-Hyperphysics-2)

Although Cooper pairing is a quantum effect, the reason for the pairing can be seen from a simplified classical explanation.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-Hyperphysics-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-3) An electron in a [metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal "Metal") normally behaves as a [free particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Free_particle "Free particle"). The electron is repelled from other electrons due to their negative [charge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charge_(physics) "Charge (physics)"), but it also attracts the positive [ions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ion "Ion") that make up the rigid lattice of the metal. This attraction distorts the ion lattice, moving the ions slightly toward the electron, increasing the positive charge density of the lattice in the vicinity. This positive charge can attract other electrons. At long distances, this attraction between electrons due to the displaced ions can overcome the electrons' repulsion due to their negative charge, and cause them to pair up. The rigorous quantum mechanical explanation shows that the effect is due to [electron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron "Electron")–[phonon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phonon "Phonon") interactions, with the phonon being the collective motion of the positively-charged lattice.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-4)

The energy of the pairing interaction is quite weak, of the order of 10−3[eV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron_volt "Electron volt"), and thermal energy can easily break the pairs. So only at low temperatures, in metal and other substrates, are a significant number of the electrons bound in Cooper pairs.

The electrons in a pair are not necessarily close together; because the interaction is long range, paired electrons may still be many hundreds of [nanometers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanometre "Nanometre") apart. This distance is usually greater than the average interelectron distance so that many Cooper pairs can occupy the same space.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-5) Electrons have [spin-1⁄2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin-%C2%BD "Spin-½"), so they are [fermions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermion "Fermion"), but the [total spin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_momentum_coupling "Angular momentum coupling") of a Cooper pair is integer (0 or 1) so it is a [composite boson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Composite_boson "Composite boson"). This means the [wave functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wave_function "Wave function") are symmetric under particle interchange. Therefore, unlike electrons, multiple Cooper pairs are allowed to be in the same quantum state, which is responsible for the phenomenon of superconductivity.

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/BEC-BCS.png/250px-BEC-BCS.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:BEC-BCS.png)

Illustration of fermionic pairings changing from a BCS superfluid with weak coupling to those of a system of diatomic molecules

The BCS theory is also applicable to other fermion systems, such as [helium-3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helium-3 "Helium-3").[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-Helium3-6) Indeed, Cooper pairing is responsible for the [superfluidity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superfluidity "Superfluidity") of helium-3 at low temperatures.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-Helium3-6) In 2008 it was proposed that pairs of [bosons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson "Boson") in an [optical lattice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_lattice "Optical lattice") may be similar to Cooper pairs.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-7)

Relationship to superconductivity
---------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cooper_pair&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Relationship to superconductivity")]

The tendency for all the Cooper pairs in a body to "[condense](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bose%E2%80%93Einstein_condensation "Bose–Einstein condensation")" into the same [ground quantum state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ground_state "Ground state") is responsible for the peculiar properties of superconductivity.

Cooper originally considered only the case of an isolated pair's formation in a metal. When one considers the more realistic state of many electronic pair formations, as is elucidated in the full BCS theory, one finds that the pairing opens a gap in the continuous spectrum of allowed energy states of the electrons, meaning that all excitations of the system must possess some minimum amount of energy. This _gap to excitations_ leads to superconductivity, since small excitations such as scattering of electrons are forbidden.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-8) The gap appears due to many-body effects between electrons feeling the attraction.

R. A. Ogg Jr., was first to suggest that electrons might act as pairs coupled by lattice vibrations in the material.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-9)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-10) This was indicated by the [isotope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotope "Isotope") effect observed in superconductors. The isotope effect showed that materials with heavier ions (different [nuclear isotopes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isotope "Isotope")) had lower superconducting transition temperatures. This can be explained by the theory of Cooper pairing: heavier ions are harder for the electrons to attract and move (how Cooper pairs are formed), which results in smaller binding energy for the pairs.

The theory of Cooper pairs is quite general and does not depend on the specific electron-phonon interaction. Condensed matter theorists have proposed pairing mechanisms based on other attractive interactions such as electron–[exciton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exciton "Exciton") interactions or electron–[plasmon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasmon "Plasmon") interactions. Currently, none of these other pairing interactions has been observed in any material.

It should be mentioned that Cooper pairing does not involve individual electrons pairing up to form "quasi-bosons". The paired states are energetically favored, and electrons go in and out of those states preferentially. This is a fine distinction that John Bardeen makes:

_"The idea of paired electrons, though not fully accurate, captures the sense of it."[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper\_pair#cite\_note-11)_
The mathematical description of the second-order coherence involved here is given by Yang.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooper_pair#cite_note-12)

# [Reason for a Cooper "Pair" Formation](/questions/410126/reason-for-a-cooper-pair-formation)

I'm an Australian high school student *(Grade 12)*. We are currently covering superconductors as a topic for our syllabus.
Can someone explain (inasmuch as an advanced *18-year-old physics student* can understand) why Cooper pairs of electrons (as per the BCS theory) only form in pairs?
As I understand it, Cooper pairs form between 2 electrons with opposite spin due to a "phonon exchange", wherein the first electron passing through the metallic lattice induces a distortion of the lattice and hence the creation of a region of higher positive charge density behind it, attracting the second electron with opposite spin (we **don't** need to know why an opposite spin is required).
However, if this second electron *also* passes through the lattice, and hence creates a region of higher positive charge density behind it, why does it not thence attract *another* electron and so on?
In other words, why do Cooper *Pairs* form and not Cooper *chains*?
Thanks for your help in advance.
\*Note: I don't mind what level of understanding your reply requires, I will just try my best to understand more complicated ideas. Thanks

![SAS2507's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/FNIxn.jpg?s=64)

## 2 Answers 2

You probably know about the Pauli principle: you cannot occupy a single quantum state by more than one electron. This is in fact related to their spin which is 1/2.

In superconductivity, two electrons team up to build a Cooper pair. The spin of the cooper pair can be 0 or 1. It therefore behaves rather like a boson than like a fermion. This is a key ingredient to superconductivity because now, the Cooper pairs can occupy the same macroscopic quantum state!

If we now think about what happens when a third electron joins the party, the spin of the composite state is half integer again and they are not allowed to team up in a macroscopic quantum state.

So what about four electrons then? Here the spin can be bosonic again. At the moment I cannot think of a reason why - in principle - there could not be a composite state made out of these four.

But already at the level of 2 electrons, the formation of Cooper pairs by the mechanism you present is a delicate business that only works at very low temperatures. The probability of 4 four electrons meeting at just the right spot with just the right momentum will be much smaller for sure. While at the same time, the energy barrier to destroy this team of 4 and break it into 2 pairs of 2 will be very low.

![sagittarius_a's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/fyfxc.jpg?s=64)

For the simplest cases, explained by the original BCS theory, all the Cooper pairs form a superconducting condensate. There are no spatially separated pairs in this condensate. They overlap. However, cooling way below the transition temperature of the material is required to convert all the conduction electrons to the condensate of pairs. A transition temperature is a temperature where the condensate becomes non-fragmented.

"Pairing" is a tendency of *spins* to become opposite, as the conduction electrons lose momentum to the crystal lattice. The more the momenta become similar (lower momentum means less kinetic energy and lower momenta cannot be as different as high momenta), the more the difference must be for spins, in order to follow the Pauli principle, which applies only for these electrons that overlap both spatially and in momenta.

There is usually no reason for Cooper pairs to form any more ordered structures even near absolute zero. It is important to remember: the lattice distortion in BCS theory is a virtual process. It does not form bound states with neither electrons nor pairs of electrons. The lattice still looks the same both below and above the superconducting transition temperature.

![Paul Kolk's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/057f4c45d42034c7cf6c1c4a398e1ad6?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG&f=y&so-version=2)

--- SOURCE 4: BCS Theory of Superconductivity: A Qualitative Overview ---
URL: https://www.bhcollege.ac.in/online/attendence/classnotes/files/1725206612.pdf

SUMMARY:
When an electron moves through the lattice, it distorts the positive ions, creating a local region of increased positive charge. This distortion can attract another electron with opposite momentum and spin, leading to the formation of a Cooper pair.

FULL CONTENT:
BCS Theory of Superconductivity: A Qualitative Overview The BCS theory, developed by John Bardeen, Leon Cooper and Robert Schrieffer in 1957, is a fundamental theory of modern condensed matter physics and provides a comprehensive explanation of superconductivity in conventional superconductors. This theory explains how certain materials can conduct electricity with zero resistance and expel magnetic fields (the Meissner effect) below a critical temperature. The Key Concepts of BCS Theory 1. Cooper Pairs: The foundation of BCS theory is the concept of Cooper pairs. In a normal conductor, electrons move independently and are subject to scattering by impurities or lattice vibrations (phonons), which causes resistance. In a superconductor, below a critical temperature (𝑇 𝑐), electrons near the Fermi surface (the highest energy electrons in a material) can form pairs known as Cooper pairs. Despite the fact that electrons are fermions (which normally repel each other due to their like charges), they can attract each other indirectly through interactions with the crystal lattice. 2. Phonon-Mediated Attraction: The attractive force between two electrons in a Cooper pair is mediated by phonons, which are quanta of lattice vibrations. When an electron moves through the lattice, it distorts the positive ions, creating a local region of increased positive charge. This distortion can attract another electron with opposite momentum and spin, leading to the formation of a Cooper pair. This pairing occurs despite the overall repulsive Coulomb interaction between the electrons because the phonon-mediated attraction is stronger at certain conditions, particularly at low temperatures. 3. Energy Gap: In the superconducting state, Cooper pairs form a collective ground state and an energy gap opens up at the Fermi surface. This gap represents the energy required to break a Cooper pair and excite an electron to a higher energy state. Because of this energy gap, there are no available states for the electrons to scatter into within the gap, meaning that scattering (and therefore resistance) is suppressed. This results in zero electrical resistance. 4. Long-Range Coherence: Cooper pairs are not localized but instead form a coherent quantum state that extends over large distances within the material. This coherence is what allows superconductors to carry current without resistance. The wavefunctions of Cooper pairs overlap, leading to a macroscopic quantum state where the pairs move in unison. This collective behaviour is key to the zero-resistance property of superconductors. 5. Meissner Effect: The BCS theory also explains the Meissner effect, where a superconductor expels an applied magnetic field from its interior when it transitions into the superconducting state. The expulsion of the magnetic field is due to the superconductor's ability to generate surface currents that exactly cancel the applied magnetic field within the material. This phenomenon is directly related to the coherence of the Cooper pairs and the energy gap. [Summary The BCS theory of superconductivity explains how, at low temperatures, electrons in a superconductor form Cooper pairs through a phonon-mediated attraction, leading to a collective quantum state. This pairing and the resulting energy gap at the Fermi surface are key to understanding the zero electrical resistance and perfect diamagnetism (Meissner effect) observed in superconductors. The BCS theory successfully describes the behaviour of conventional (low-temperature) superconductors and laid the foundation for the modern understanding of superconductivity.]

--- SOURCE 5: Cooper Pairs and the BCS Theory of Superconductivity - HyperPhysics ---
URL: http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html

SUMMARY:
Cooper Pairs ============ The transition of a metal from the normal to the superconducting state has the nature of a condensation of the electrons into a state which leaves a band gapabove them. As strange as such an interaction seems, it is experimentally supported by the isotope effect and the evidence for a condensation to a boson-like state at the critical temperature for superconductivity.Index Superconductivity concepts Reference Rohlf,Ch 15 This supported the BCS theory of lattice coupling of electron pairs. This was an important clue in the process of developing the BCS theory because it suggested lattice coupling, and in the quantum treatment suggested that phonons were involved.Index Superconductivity concepts Reference Rohlf,Ch 15

FULL CONTENT:
Cooper Pairs and the BCS Theory of Superconductivity
===============

Cooper Pairs ============ The behavior of [superconductors](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/scond.html#c1) suggests that electron pairs are coupling over a range of hundreds of nanometers, three orders of magnitude larger than the lattice spacing. Called [Cooper pairs](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c2), these coupled electrons can take the character of a boson and condense into the ground state.![Image 1](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs6.png) This pair condensation is the basis for the [BCS theory](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/bcs.html#c1) of superconductivity. The effective net attraction between the normally repulsive electrons produces a pair binding energy on the order of milli-electron volts, enough to keep them paired at extremely low temperatures. [Further discussion](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c2)[Model of the attractive mechanism](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c3)[Index](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hframe.html) [Superconductivity concepts](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/supcon.html#c1) Reference [Rohlf](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/solref.html#rohlf),Ch 15
[HyperPhysics](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hph.html) ***** [Condensed Matter](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/solcon.html)_R Nave_[Go Back](javascript:history.go(-1))

Cooper Pairs ============ The transition of a metal from the normal to the superconducting state has the nature of a condensation of the electrons into a state which leaves a [band gap](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/bcs.html#c3)above them. This kind of condensation is seen with [superfluid](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/lhel.html#c3) helium, but helium is made up of [bosons](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/particles/spinc.html#c3) -- multiple electrons can't collect into a single state because of the [Pauli exclusion principle](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/pauli.html#c2). Froehlich was first to suggest that the electrons act as pairs coupled by lattice vibrations in the material. This coupling is viewed as an exchange of [phonons](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/phonon.html#c1), phonons being the quanta of lattice vibration energy. Experimental corroboration of an interaction with the lattice was provided by the [isotope effect](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c5) on the superconducting transition temperature. The boson-like behavior of such electron pairs was further investigated by Cooper and they are called "Cooper pairs". The condensation of Cooper pairs is the foundation of the [BCS theory](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/bcs.html#c1) of [superconductivity](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/scond.html#c1). [Resistance diagram](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/scex.html#c2)Phase diagram[Index](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hframe.html) [Superconductivity concepts](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/supcon.html#c1) Reference [Rohlf](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/solref.html#rohlf),Ch 15
[HyperPhysics](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hph.html) ***** [Condensed Matter](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/solcon.html)_R Nave_[Go Back](javascript:history.go(-1))

![Image 2](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs7.png)A passing electron attracts the lattice, causing a slight ripple toward its path. A model of [Cooper pair](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c1) attraction =================================================================================================== ![Image 3](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs8.png)Another electron passing in the opposite direction is attracted to that displacement. [Further discussion of mechanism](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c4)[Index](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hframe.html) [Superconductivity concepts](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/supcon.html#c1) Reference [Rohlf](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/solref.html#rohlf),Ch 15
[HyperPhysics](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hph.html) ***** [Condensed Matter](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/solcon.html)_R Nave_[Go Back](javascript:history.go(-1))

Model of Pair Attraction ======================== ![Image 4](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs9.png) ![Image 5](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs9b.png)A visual model of the [Cooper pair](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c1) attraction has a passing electron which attracts the lattice, causing a slight ripple toward its path. Another electron passing in the opposite direction is attracted to that displacement. This constitutes a coupling between electrons which can be depicted in a [Feynman diagram](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/particles/expar.html#c2). As strange as such an interaction seems, it is experimentally supported by the [isotope effect](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c5) and the evidence for a [condensation to a boson-like state](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c2) at the critical temperature for superconductivity.![Image 6](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs10.png)[Index](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hframe.html) [Superconductivity concepts](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/supcon.html#c1) Reference [Rohlf](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/solref.html#rohlf),Ch 15
[HyperPhysics](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hph.html) ***** [Condensed Matter](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/solcon.html)_R Nave_[Go Back](javascript:history.go(-1))

Isotope Effect, Mercury ======================= If electrical conduction in mercury were purely electronic, there should be no dependence upon the nuclear masses. This dependence of the [critical temperature](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/scond.html#c2)for superconductivity upon isotopic mass was the first direct evidence for interaction between the electrons and the lattice. This supported the [BCS theory](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/bcs.html#c1) of lattice coupling of [electron pairs](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/coop.html#c1). ![Image 7](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/imgsol/bcs11.png)It is quite remarkable that an electrical phenomenon like the transition to zero resistivity should involve a purely mechanical property of the lattice. Since a change in the critical temperature involves a change in the energy environment associated with the superconducting transition, this suggests that part of the energy is being used to move the atoms of the lattice since the energy depends upon the mass of the lattice. This indicates that lattice vibrations are a part of the superconducting process. This was an important clue in the process of developing the BCS theory because it suggested lattice coupling, and in the quantum treatment suggested that phonons were involved.[Index](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hframe.html) [Superconductivity concepts](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/supcon.html#c1) Reference [Rohlf](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Solids/solref.html#rohlf),Ch 15
[HyperPhysics](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/hph.html) ***** [Condensed Matter](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/solcon.html)_R Nave_[Go Back](javascript:history.go(-1))

enriched_points.expanded_content: The BCS theory, formulated by Bardeen, Cooper, and Schrieffer, explains superconductivity through the formation of Cooper pairs, resulting in zero electrical resistance in certain materials.
Cooper pairs consist of two electrons bound via lattice vibrations, allowing them to occupy the same quantum state, overcoming their natural repulsion due to their opposite spins.
Lattice vibrations, or phonons, facilitate attractive interactions between electrons, creating regions of positive charge density that assist in the formation of Cooper pairs at low temperatures.
The formation of Cooper pairs opens an energy gap at the Fermi surface, preventing excitations that lead to scattering, crucial for maintaining superconductivity and zero resistance.
Cooper pairs exhibit macroscopic quantum phenomena, allowing large-scale coherence across the material, crucial for the unique properties of superconductors, such as persistent currents.
